epigraph.text:
It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of 
kings is to search things out.

content.text:
Some integrals have *no* closed-form antiderivative. The function
$e^{-x^{2}}$ is perfectly well-defined, continuous, and smooth—yet no
combination of elementary functions equals $\int e^{-x^{2}}\dx$. And this
is not an obscure curiosity: the integral
$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^{2}}\dx = \sqrt{\pi}$ lies at the heart of
probability theory and statistics. Every bell curve, every normal
distribution depends on it.

Other times we have no formula at all—only data points collected from an
experiment. A physicist measures velocity at discrete times; an engineer
records stress at measured positions. To find the total displacement or the
total force, we must integrate a function known only through a table.

In such situations, we turn to **numerical methods**: systematic
approximations that estimate $\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx$ to any desired accuracy.

Recall from Chapter 6 that $\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx$ is defined as a limit of
Riemann sums. Partition $[a,b]$ into $n$ equal subintervals of width
$\Delta x = (b-a)/n$ with endpoints $x_{0}=a, x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}=b$.
The **left** and **right Riemann sums** are
\[
 L_{n} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}f(x_{i})\,\Delta x,
 \qquad
 R_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}f(x_{i})\,\Delta x.
\]
Both converge to the integral as $n \to \infty$, but for moderate $n$ their
accuracy is limited. We can do better.

content.caption: Left and Right Riemann Sums
content.alt: Side-by-side comparison of left and right Riemann sums. Left panel shows the left Riemann sum Lₙ, where rectangles use the function value at the left endpoint of each subinterval—this underestimates the area for an increasing function. Right panel shows the right Riemann sum Rₙ, where rectangles use the function value at the right endpoint—this overestimates the area for an increasing function. Both panels show a smooth curve with rectangles underneath.

content.text:
Instead of approximating $f$ by a constant on each subinterval (a rectangle),
we approximate by a *straight line* (a trapezoid). On $[x_{i},
x_{i+1}]$, the trapezoid has area
\[
 \frac{\Delta x}{2}\bigl[f(x_{i}) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr].
\]
Summing over all subintervals gives the **Trapezoid Rule**.

content.caption: The Trapezoid Rule
content.alt: Diagram showing the trapezoid rule for numerical integration. A smooth curve f(x) is approximated by straight line segments connecting consecutive points (x₀, f(x₀)), (x₁, f(x₁)), etc. The area under these line segments forms trapezoids. Each trapezoid has width Δx. The shaded purple region shows how the trapezoids approximate the area under the actual curve. A legend distinguishes the actual curve (solid red) from the linear approximation (dashed purple).

content.text:
Note the pattern: the first and last function values appear once; all
interior values appear twice. This is simply the average of the left and
right Riemann sums: $T_{n} = (L_{n}+R_{n})/2$.

Why stop at straight lines? If we approximate $f$ by a *parabola*
on each pair of subintervals, we get a far more accurate method.

content.title: Simpson's Rule
content.content:
With $n$ **even** and $\Delta x = (b-a)/n$:
\[
 S_{n} = \frac{\Delta x}{3}
 \bigl[f(x_{0}) + 4f(x_{1}) + 2f(x_{2}) + 4f(x_{3}) + 2f(x_{4})
 + \cdots + 4f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_{n})\bigr].
\]
The coefficients follow the pattern $1, 4, 2, 4, 2, \ldots, 4, 2, 4, 1$.
content.label: Theorem

content.caption: Simpson's Rule
content.alt: Diagram showing Simpson's rule for numerical integration. A smooth curve f(x) is approximated by parabolic segments that pass through consecutive triples of points. The shaded orange region shows the parabolic approximations. Above each point, the coefficient weights are shown: 1, 4, 2, 4, 1 for five points, illustrating the alternating pattern. Simpson's rule achieves much higher accuracy than the trapezoid rule because parabolas fit smooth curves better than straight lines.

content.title: $n$ must be even for Simpson's Rule
content.content:
Simpson's Rule fits a parabola to each consecutive triple of points,
requiring an even number of subintervals.

content.title: Simpson's Rule
content.solution:
Using the same partition as Example [ex:trap-basic]:
$$\begin{aligned}
S_{4} &= \frac{0.5}{3}\bigl[1 + 4(0.6667) + 2(0.5)
 + 4(0.4) + 0.3333\bigr] \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{0.5}{3}\bigl[1 + 2.6667 + 1 + 1.6 + 0.3333\bigr]
 = \frac{0.5}{3}(6.6) \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{3.3}{3} = 1.1.
\end{aligned}$$
The exact value is $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986$. Simpson's Rule gives an error
of only $0.0014$—more than ten times better than the Trapezoid Rule with
the same $n$.

content.title: Comparing methods
content.problem:
For $\displaystyle\defint{0}{1}e^{-x^{2}}\dx$ (no closed-form
antiderivative!), approximate using $n=4$ with each method.

content.text: How accurate are these approximations? The following bounds tell us.

content.title: Error Bounds for Numerical Integration
content.content:
Suppose $f$ has continuous derivatives on $[a,b]$. Let
$\Delta x = (b-a)/n$.

**Trapezoid Rule:** If $\abs{f''(x)} \leq K_{2}$ on $[a,b]$, then
\[
 \abs{\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx - T_{n}}
 \leq \frac{K_{2}(b-a)^{3}}{12n^{2}}.
\]

**Simpson's Rule:** If $\abs{f^{(4)}(x)} \leq K_{4}$ on $[a,b]$,
then
\[
 \abs{\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx - S_{n}}
 \leq \frac{K_{4}(b-a)^{5}}{180n^{4}}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Notice the key difference: the Trapezoid error decreases like $1/n^{2}$
(doubling $n$ cuts the error by a factor of $4$), while Simpson's error
decreases like $1/n^{4}$ (doubling $n$ cuts the error by a factor
of $16$). This explains why Simpson's Rule is so much more efficient.

content.title: How many subintervals?
content.problem:
How many subintervals are needed to approximate
$\displaystyle\defint{1}{3}\frac{1}{x}\dx$ to within $10^{-6}$ using
(a) the Trapezoid Rule and (b) Simpson's Rule?
content.solution:
For $f(x) = 1/x$ on $[1,3]$:
$f''(x) = 2/x^{3}$, so $\abs{f''(x)} \leq 2$ on $[1,3]$.
$f^{(4)}(x) = 24/x^{5}$, so $\abs{f^{(4)}(x)} \leq 24$ on $[1,3]$.

**(a) Trapezoid:** We need
$\dfrac{2 \cdot 8}{12n^{2}} < 10^{-6}$, i.e.,
$n^{2} > \dfrac{16}{12 \cdot 10^{-6}} = 1.33\times 10^{6}$,
so $n > 1155$. We need $n \geq 1156$.

**(b) Simpson's:** We need
$\dfrac{24 \cdot 32}{180n^{4}} < 10^{-6}$, i.e.,
$n^{4} > \dfrac{768}{180 \cdot 10^{-6}} = 4.27\times 10^{6}$,
so $n > 45.4$. We need $n \geq 46$ (even), so $n = 46$.

Simpson's Rule achieves the same accuracy with $46$ subintervals instead of
$1{,}156$—a dramatic difference.

content.title: Thomas Simpson (1710--1761)
content.content:
Thomas Simpson, a self-taught English mathematician, published the rule
bearing his name in 1743, though Newton and Cotes had discovered equivalent
formulas earlier. Simpson came from a weaving family and taught himself
mathematics from textbooks. His story reminds us that mathematical talent
knows no social boundary—a truth consistent with the imago Dei, the image
of God in every human person.

marginNotes.text:
Know well the condition of your flocks, and
give attention to your herds.

A false balance is an abomination to the
Lord, but a just weight is his delight.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on the
shoulders of giants.
